countries: United States Minor Outlying Islands (Line Islands)
French Polynesia (Society Islands, Austral Islands, Rapa)
nativeRange.habitat: Inhabits clear-water coral reefs from shallow water to depths of at least about 30 m; generally observed grazing surface algal films and detritus from hard substrate of lagoon and seaward reef areas

decorPreferences: live rock with biofilm and algal turf for grazing
rockwork crevices and overhangs for shelter
long horizontal footprint preferred over tall depth
sand bed for natural grazing behaviour

diet.notes: Uses individually movable bristle-like teeth to sift detritus and graze surface algal films from live rock and other hard substrate. Marine-based seaweed (nori) and spirulina-rich herbivore preparations are generally advised at least three times per week. Has been reported to accept algae-based flakes/pellets, frozen vegetable preparations, and small meaty items (mysis, brine, krill) in mixed-community feedings; vitamin-supplemented diets are commonly recommended to reduce risk of Head and Lateral Line Erosion.

goodWith: non-Ctenochaetus tangs (with caution and ample space)
avoidWith: similarly shaped or similarly coloured surgeonfish

summary: A small Central Pacific bristletooth surgeonfish with a dark brown to purplish body and a distinctive abrupt white caudal peduncle and tail. Generally considered an efficient, reef-safe algae and detritus grazer for moderately sized reef systems.
careNotes:
The White Tail Bristletooth Tang (Ctenochaetus flavicauda) is a small Central Pacific surgeonfish named for the abruptly white caudal peduncle and tail set against a deep brown to purplish body. Adult body length is generally reported around 14–17 cm; FishBase records a maximum of 16.0 cm TL, while retail sources span roughly 5–7 inches (~13–18 cm).

A tank around 400 L (~105 gal) with a long horizontal footprint is generally advised. Retail sources span 70 gal (265 L, LiveAquaria) to 75 gal (Top Shelf Aquatics) to 90 gal (340 L, Aquatics Unlimited) to 100–120 gal (~380–450 L, additional hobby guides). The cautious-larger choice reflects that bristletooth tangs are active grazers throughout the day, can show territorial behaviour toward similar species, and benefit from open swimming space combined with extensive live rock for natural grazing routes.

Water parameters should be stable and well-oxygenated within standard reef ranges (SG ~1.022–1.026, pH 8.1–8.4, dKH 8–12). Temperature is commonly recommended around 23–27°C; LiveAquaria and Aquatics Unlimited specify 72–78°F (22–26°C), Top Shelf Aquatics 74–78°F (23–26°C), and FishBase's modelled preferred range is ~24.4–28.8°C with a mean near 26.6°C — averaged to a practical 23–27°C aquarium target.

Diet should lean heavily on marine algae. Dried seaweed (nori) and spirulina-based foods at least three times per week are generally advised; vitamin-rich diets are commonly suggested to reduce risk of Head and Lateral Line Erosion (HLLE), a condition this species (like most Acanthurids) is reported to develop in poor water quality or on a vitamin-deficient diet.

The species is generally considered reef-safe; reports of coral nipping are uncommon and tend to involve under-fed individuals. It is best kept singly per tank — conspecific aggression toward other Ctenochaetus and similarly shaped tangs has been reported as high — and is generally peaceable with non-tang reef fish.

As an acanthurid, this species possesses sharp caudal-peduncle blades (the 'scalpel') used for defence. They are not venomous but can inflict lacerations on handlers and tankmates during conflicts; handle with care during transfers.

Cross-source notes: Max size ranged from ~13 cm (Top Shelf '5 in') to ~18 cm (Reef Chasers 'up to 7 in'), with FishBase at 16.0 cm TL — averaged to 14–17 cm. Tank minimum spread 70–120 gal across hobby sources; per the cautious-larger rule rounded to 400 L reflecting the species' active grazing behaviour and semi-territorial nature. Temperature spread 22–29°C across sources; averaged to 23–27°C. Care level was rated 'moderate' by LiveAquaria and Aquatics Unlimited — recorded as intermediate. Native range cited variously as Tahiti (LiveAquaria/Top Shelf Aquatics), Marshall Islands (Reef Chasers), and US Minor Outlying / Line / Society / Austral / Rapa Islands (FishBase) — consolidated to Eastern and Western Central Pacific.
breedingNotes: Captive breeding has not been documented in available hobby sources. Like most acanthurids, Ctenochaetus flavicauda is presumed to be a pelagic broadcast spawner in the wild, with planktonic larvae; spawning in home aquaria is considered highly unlikely given tank-size and pair-bonding constraints.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Species product page for White Tail Bristletooth Tang. Provided max size (6.5 in / ~16.5 cm), tank minimum (70 gal / 265 L), temperature (72–78°F / 22–26°C), pH (8.1–8.4), SG (1.020–1.025), dKH (8–12), care level (moderate), diet (herbivore, seaweed 3x/week), temperament (semi-aggressive toward tangs, peaceful otherwise), reef compatibility (yes), family (Acanthuridae), and native range (Tahiti, South Pacific). Reached via the tangs-surgeons collection root supplied in the brief.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked species identity (Ctenochaetus flavicauda Fowler, 1938), family (Acanthuridae) and order (Acanthuriformes), max size (16.0 cm TL), depth range (1–30 m), preferred temperature (24.4–28.8°C, mean 26.6°C), Central Pacific native range (US Minor Outlying Islands; Line, Society, Rapa and Austral Islands), habitat (coral reefs, shallow to 30 m), IUCN status (Least Concern, 2010), and diagnostic feature (posterior caudal peduncle and fin abruptly white in life). Reef2Reef wiki had no species-specific page for C. flavicauda, so FishBase served as the first verified secondary.

additional.name: Aquatics Unlimited
additional.notes: Hobby retailer secondary used for tank-size triangulation. Confirmed max size (~6 in / 15 cm), minimum tank (90 gal / ~340 L, larger than LiveAquaria's 70 gal), water parameters (72–78°F, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.020–1.025, dKH 8–12), semi-aggressive toward genus / peaceful with others, reef-safe, and Tahiti / South Pacific native range.

additional.name: Top Shelf Aquatics
additional.notes: Third hobby source consulted for sizing and temperament triangulation. Stated max size 5–6 in (~13–15 cm), minimum 75 gal (~284 L), temperature 74–78°F (23–26°C), SG 1.024–1.026, generally peaceful but territorial toward other tangs, reef-safe via specialised teeth, and Central Pacific (Marshall Islands) native range.

fish.breedingNotes: No captive breeding records found in available hobby sources. Like most acanthurids, presumed pelagic broadcast spawner in the wild; spawning in aquaria is considered highly unlikely.
